events.text: Notable negative: despite the server-CPU shortage, Intel's CFO 'shot down' the idea of ordering more EUV tools, saying it has sufficient capacity; BofA models Intel taking ~1 additional High-NA tool in 2026 and ~1 in 2027, then 3 systems for the 14A ramp in 2029 (could be zero).

Revenue $13.6B, +7% y/y, beating the guide midpoint by $1.4B (sixth straight beat); non-GAAP GM 41% (~650bps above guide); non-GAAP EPS $0.29. But GAAP showed an operating loss of $(3.1B) and EPS $(0.73) on a $4.07B restructuring charge. Q2 FY26 guidance: revenue $13.8–14.8B (+2-9% q/q).

Laid out the $56 PT framework: Intel Products at ~20x 2028E EPS + Intel Foundry external book at ~10x P/S on $3B (2027)/$6B (2028) of external foundry revenue. Earnings build ~$1.00 (2026)/$1.50 (2027)/$2+ (2028); $4 bull case in 2028 maps to ~$80. SOTP, not a multiple. BofA maintains Underperform.

18A with yields ~2-3 months ahead of internal benchmarks; 14A with 'maturity, yield and performance outpacing 18A' at the equivalent point, with external customers in the definition phase. EMIB/EMIB-T packaging re-rated from 'hundreds of millions' to 'billions/year' per engagement, ramp visible in 2027. Capex flat y/y but equipment +25% y/y.

Intel is the front-runner in High-NA EUV (vs TSMC's deliberately slow adoption); High-NA 'done right' could be a differentiator/leapfrog: ~75% fewer process steps, relaxed design rules, lower parasitic capacitance. The feared field-stitching problem proved 'not that serious'; the High-NA line validated as 'healthy and ready' before the expert's retirement in Sep/2025.
UBS models Intel's server-CPU unit share falling from 60% (2025) to 29% (2030), with ARM rising 16%→42% and AMD 24%→29% — the same structural erosion BofA flagged on a value basis.

'Extremely impressed' with Lip-Bu Tan's first event for Wall Street: 18A yields improving ~6-7 percentage points per month; 'multiple engagements on 14A, some on 18A-P', with commitments locked only in advanced packaging; possible 14A customer announcements at the 2H-2026 analyst day. Caution: the market is not pricing execution risk, 14A HVM only ~CY29.

To model GM use ~40-60% incremental drop-through; applied literally, exit-2026 GM lands in the low-40s; mid-40s is the bar to 'really believe' in the turnaround. On 18A, the margin drag stays neutral to the product business by year-end but remains a manufacturing headwind — pushed back on the '18A accretive by year-end' read. IFS break-even at exit-2027, pushed out with each new customer.
EMIB production yield is only ~60-70% vs TSMC's CoWoS 95%+, and EMIB-T is 'not yet ready to enter mass production', so MediaTek/Google's priority remains TSMC CoWoS-L despite interest in EMIB-T.

Frames Q1-26 as an inflection: Intel raised its FY26 capex guide to flat y/y (the first guide-up of the saga) and bought back Apollo's 49% of Fab 34 for $14.2B (closed Apr-8-2026). With ~$51.5B of debt post-bridge, SemiAnalysis argues equity is now the cheapest capital and Intel should raise ~$40B to fund the Terafab ramp and the N3 overflow opportunity; estimates a $20-35B financing hole.

Terafab requesting ~$5B of WFE for a desired pilot line within 12 months; Terafab WFE could reach high-single-digit / up to ~$10B by 2028. In the CPU debate Arcuri prefers AMD/ARM to Intel — skeptical on the product roadmap — though 'foundry news flow remains very good' (4 potential foundry deals this fall); does not think Intel needs a large capital raise.

Bullish on the MS bus tour takeaways (INTC/MRVL/ALAB), citing the CEO's commitment to foundry and a positive view on microprocessors; INTC included among the bullish names of the West Coast Semis bus tour.
Raised the PT to $100 (from $65), Market-Perform, on 18A-P process milestones + the read-through on CPU TAM and IFS packaging optionality. INTC +2.6% pre-market on Jun-17 on the 18A-P update.
INTC +8-9% on the chip deal with Apple — a sentiment inflection.

Contrarian read from an expert at one of the largest ODMs (a CPU buyer): Intel's x86 server share at 50% (2024)→55% (2025)→~60% (2026). Mechanism: Intel moved laptop/desktop designs to 18A, lifting 18A utilization/yields and freeing capacity on existing nodes for Xeon — explaining the surprise margin beat; Intel 'appears less capacity-constrained than AMD'. Both are expected to raise server-CPU prices +15-20% in H2.

cells: CCG $7.7B, -6% q/q; FY26 PC TAM down low-double-digit%
DCAI $5.1B, +7% q/q, +22% y/y; server CPU double-digit growth
Yields ~2-3 months ahead; fastest ramps in 5 years
Volume gated on binding commitment; vol 2028-29
Maturity/yield outpacing 18A; customers in definition phase
Op loss $(3.2B); FY25 gross capex cut to $18B
Op loss $(2.5B); 2026 capex 'flat to down slightly'
Op loss $(2.4B); capex flat y/y, equipment +25% y/y
